questions.question: What is Heron’s Formula used for?
questions.correct_answer: To find the area of a triangle when its three sides are known
questions.explanation: It allows calculation of area without knowing the height of the triangle.

questions.question: State Heron’s Formula.
questions.explanation: s is the semi-perimeter of the triangle, and a, b, c are its sides.

questions.question: How is the semi-perimeter calculated?
questions.explanation: It is half of the sum of all three sides.

questions.question: What is the area of a triangle with sides 5 cm, 6 cm, and 7 cm using Heron’s Formula?
questions.explanation: Step-by-step calculation using semi-perimeter and formula.

questions.question: Can Heron’s Formula be applied to all triangles?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, for any triangle where all sides are known
questions.explanation: It works for scalene, isosceles, and equilateral triangles.

questions.question: What is the area of an equilateral triangle with side a using Heron’s Formula?
questions.explanation: For equilateral triangles, formula simplifies using semi-perimeter s = 3a/2.

questions.question: What is the advantage of using Heron’s Formula?
questions.correct_answer: No need to calculate height; uses only side lengths
questions.explanation: Simplifies area calculation especially when height is difficult to measure.

questions.question: How do you check if three sides can form a triangle before using Heron’s Formula?
questions.correct_answer: The sum of any two sides must be greater than the third side
questions.explanation: This is the triangle inequality theorem.

questions.question: State a real-life application of Heron’s Formula.
questions.correct_answer: Calculating area of land plots with triangular shape
questions.explanation: Useful in surveying and construction when sides are known.

questions.question: How does Heron’s Formula relate to the semi-perimeter?
questions.correct_answer: Area depends on semi-perimeter and differences (s−a), (s−b), (s−c)
questions.explanation: It shows how area decreases if any side is smaller relative to s.

notes.title: Heron’s Formula
notes.content: Area = √[s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)], s = (a+b+c)/2; works for all triangles.

notes.title: Semi-perimeter
notes.content: s is half the sum of triangle sides: s = (a+b+c)/2.

notes.title: Triangle Inequality Check
notes.content: Sum of any two sides must be greater than the third side.

notes.title: Equilateral Triangle Area
notes.content: Area = (√3/4) × a², derived from Heron’s Formula.

facts.fact: Heron’s Formula works for scalene, isosceles, and equilateral triangles.
Semi-perimeter s is always greater than each side.
Triangle inequality must hold to form a triangle.
No height is required; only side lengths are needed.
Area decreases if one side becomes much smaller compared to semi-perimeter.
